name: flup6

metadata.name: flup6
classifiers: Topic :: Software Development :: Libraries :: Python Modules

documentation:
# flup6

This is a fork of Allan Saddi's flup package, it is intended to support both Python2
and Python3 from single code-base.

## Installation

```bash
$ pip install flup6
```

## Status

Package works on Python 3.3

## License

BSD

modules.name: flup.server.ajp_fork
documentation:
ajp - an AJP 1.3/WSGI gateway.

For more information about AJP and AJP connectors for your web server, see
<http://jakarta.apache.org/tomcat/connectors-doc/>.

For more information about the Web Server Gateway Interface, see
<http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0333.html>.

Example usage:

  #!/usr/bin/env python
  import sys
  from myapplication import app # Assume app is your WSGI application object
  from ajp import WSGIServer
  ret = WSGIServer(app).run()
  sys.exit(ret and 42 or 0)

See the documentation for WSGIServer for more information.

About the bit of logic at the end:
Upon receiving SIGHUP, the python script will exit with status code 42. This
can be used by a wrapper script to determine if the python script should be
re-run. When a SIGINT or SIGTERM is received, the script exits with status
code 0, possibly indicating a normal exit.

Example wrapper script:

  #!/bin/sh
  STATUS=42
  while test $STATUS -eq 42; do
    python "$@" that_script_above.py
    STATUS=$?
  done

Example workers.properties (for mod_jk):

  worker.list=foo
  worker.foo.port=8009
  worker.foo.host=localhost
  worker.foo.type=ajp13

Example httpd.conf (for mod_jk):

  JkWorkersFile /path/to/workers.properties
  JkMount /* foo

Note that if you mount your ajp application anywhere but the root ("/"), you
SHOULD specifiy scriptName to the WSGIServer constructor. This will ensure
that SCRIPT_NAME/PATH_INFO are correctly deduced.
Copyright (c) 2005, 2006 Allan Saddi <allan@saddi.com>
All rights reserved.

functions.name: flup.server.ajp_fork.test_app
documentation: Probably not the most efficient example.

classes.name: flup.server.ajp_fork.WSGIServer

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_fork.WSGIServer.__init__
documentation:
scriptName is the initial portion of the URL path that "belongs"
to your application. It is used to determine PATH_INFO (which doesn't
seem to be passed in). An empty scriptName means your application
is mounted at the root of your virtual host.

environ, which must be a dictionary, can contain any additional
environment variables you want to pass to your application.

bindAddress is the address to bind to, which must be a tuple of
length 2. The first element is a string, which is the host name
or IPv4 address of a local interface. The 2nd element is the port
number.

allowedServers must be None or a list of strings representing the
IPv4 addresses of servers allowed to connect. None means accept
connections from anywhere.

loggingLevel sets the logging level of the module-level logger.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_fork.WSGIServer.run
documentation:
Main loop. Call this after instantiating WSGIServer. SIGHUP, SIGINT,
SIGQUIT, SIGTERM cause it to cleanup and return. (If a SIGHUP
is caught, this method returns True. Returns False otherwise.)

AJP1.3/WSGI server. Runs your WSGI application as a persistant program
that understands AJP1.3. Opens up a TCP socket, binds it, and then
waits for forwarded requests from your webserver.

Why AJP? Two good reasons are that AJP provides load-balancing and
fail-over support. Personally, I just wanted something new to
implement. :)

Of course you will need an AJP1.3 connector for your webserver (e.g.
mod_jk) - see <http://jakarta.apache.org/tomcat/connectors-doc/>.

modules.name: flup.server.scgi_fork
documentation:
scgi - an SCGI/WSGI gateway.

For more information about SCGI and mod_scgi for Apache1/Apache2, see
<http://www.mems-exchange.org/software/scgi/>.

For more information about the Web Server Gateway Interface, see
<http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0333.html>.

Example usage:

  #!/usr/bin/env python
  import sys
  from myapplication import app # Assume app is your WSGI application object
  from scgi import WSGIServer
  ret = WSGIServer(app).run()
  sys.exit(ret and 42 or 0)

See the documentation for WSGIServer for more information.

About the bit of logic at the end:
Upon receiving SIGHUP, the python script will exit with status code 42. This
can be used by a wrapper script to determine if the python script should be
re-run. When a SIGINT or SIGTERM is received, the script exits with status
code 0, possibly indicating a normal exit.

Example wrapper script:

  #!/bin/sh
  STATUS=42
  while test $STATUS -eq 42; do
    python "$@" that_script_above.py
    STATUS=$?
  done

functions.name: flup.server.scgi_fork.test_app
documentation: Probably not the most efficient example.

classes.name: flup.server.scgi_fork.WSGIServer

methods.name: flup.server.scgi_fork.WSGIServer.__init__
documentation:
scriptName is the initial portion of the URL path that "belongs"
to your application. It is used to determine PATH_INFO (which doesn't
seem to be passed in). An empty scriptName means your application
is mounted at the root of your virtual host.

environ, which must be a dictionary, can contain any additional
environment variables you want to pass to your application.

bindAddress is the address to bind to, which must be a string or
a tuple of length 2. If a tuple, the first element must be a string,
which is the host name or IPv4 address of a local interface. The
2nd element of the tuple is the port number. If a string, it will
be interpreted as a filename and a UNIX socket will be opened.

If binding to a UNIX socket, umask may be set to specify what
the umask is to be changed to before the socket is created in the
filesystem. After the socket is created, the previous umask is
restored.

allowedServers must be None or a list of strings representing the
IPv4 addresses of servers allowed to connect. None means accept
connections from anywhere.

loggingLevel sets the logging level of the module-level logger.

methods.name: flup.server.scgi_fork.WSGIServer.run
documentation:
Main loop. Call this after instantiating WSGIServer. SIGHUP, SIGINT,
SIGQUIT, SIGTERM cause it to cleanup and return. (If a SIGHUP
is caught, this method returns True. Returns False otherwise.)

SCGI/WSGI server. For information about SCGI (Simple Common Gateway
Interface), see <http://www.mems-exchange.org/software/scgi/>.

This server is similar to SWAP <http://www.idyll.org/~t/www-tools/wsgi/>,
another SCGI/WSGI server.

It differs from SWAP in that it isn't based on scgi.scgi_server and
therefore, it allows me to implement concurrency using threads. (Also,
this server was written from scratch and really has no other depedencies.)
Which server to use really boils down to whether you want multithreading
or forking. (But as an aside, I've found scgi.scgi_server's implementation
of preforking to be quite superior. So if your application really doesn't
mind running in multiple processes, go use SWAP. ;)

functions.name: flup.server.scgi_base.recvall
documentation:
Attempts to receive length bytes from a socket, blocking if necessary.
(Socket may be blocking or non-blocking.)

functions.name: flup.server.scgi_base.readNetstring
documentation: Attempt to read a netstring from a socket.

classes.name: flup.server.scgi_base.ProtocolError
documentation:
Exception raised when the server does something unexpected or
sends garbled data. Usually leads to a Connection closing.

classes.name: flup.server.scgi_base.StdoutWrapper
documentation: Wrapper for sys.stdout so we know if data has actually been written.

classes.name: flup.server.scgi_base.Request
documentation:
Encapsulates data related to a single request.

Public attributes:
  environ - Environment variables from web server.
  stdin - File-like object representing the request body.
  stdout - File-like object for writing the response.

classes.name: flup.server.scgi_base.Connection
documentation:
Represents a single client (web server) connection. A single request
is handled, after which the socket is closed.

methods.name: flup.server.scgi_base.BaseSCGIServer.__init__
documentation:
scriptName is the initial portion of the URL path that "belongs"
to your application. It is used to determine PATH_INFO (which doesn't
seem to be passed in). An empty scriptName means your application
is mounted at the root of your virtual host.

environ, which must be a dictionary, can contain any additional
environment variables you want to pass to your application.

Set multithreaded to False if your application is not thread-safe.

Set multiprocess to True to explicitly set wsgi.multiprocess to
True. (Only makes sense with threaded servers.)

bindAddress is the address to bind to, which must be a string or
a tuple of length 2. If a tuple, the first element must be a string,
which is the host name or IPv4 address of a local interface. The
2nd element of the tuple is the port number. If a string, it will
be interpreted as a filename and a UNIX socket will be opened.

If binding to a UNIX socket, umask may be set to specify what
the umask is to be changed to before the socket is created in the
filesystem. After the socket is created, the previous umask is
restored.

allowedServers must be None or a list of strings representing the
IPv4 addresses of servers allowed to connect. None means accept
connections from anywhere. By default, it is a list containing
the single item '127.0.0.1'.

loggingLevel sets the logging level of the module-level logger.

methods.name: flup.server.scgi_base.BaseSCGIServer._setupSocket
documentation: Creates and binds the socket for communication with the server.

methods.name: flup.server.scgi_base.BaseSCGIServer._cleanupSocket
documentation: Closes the main socket.

methods.name: flup.server.scgi_base.BaseSCGIServer.handler
documentation:
WSGI handler. Sets up WSGI environment, calls the application,
and sends the application's response.

methods.name: flup.server.scgi_base.BaseSCGIServer._sanitizeEnv
documentation: Fill-in/deduce missing values in environ.

methods.name: flup.server.scgi_base.BaseSCGIServer.error
documentation:
Override to provide custom error handling. Ideally, however,
all errors should be caught at the application level.

methods.name: flup.server.threadedserver.ThreadedServer.run
documentation:
The main loop. Pass a socket that is ready to accept() client
connections. Return value will be True or False indiciating whether
or not the loop was exited due to SIGHUP.

methods.name: flup.server.threadedserver.ThreadedServer._mainloopPeriodic
documentation:
Called with just about each iteration of the main loop. Meant to
be overridden.

methods.name: flup.server.threadedserver.ThreadedServer._exit
documentation:
Protected convenience method for subclasses to force an exit. Not
really thread-safe, which is why it isn't public.

methods.name: flup.server.threadedserver.ThreadedServer._isClientAllowed
documentation: Override to provide access control.

methods.name: flup.server.threadedserver.ThreadedServer._hupHandler
documentation: Signal handlers

modules.name: flup.server.ajp
documentation:
ajp - an AJP 1.3/WSGI gateway.

For more information about AJP and AJP connectors for your web server, see
<http://jakarta.apache.org/tomcat/connectors-doc/>.

For more information about the Web Server Gateway Interface, see
<http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0333.html>.

Example usage:

  #!/usr/bin/env python
  import sys
  from myapplication import app # Assume app is your WSGI application object
  from ajp import WSGIServer
  ret = WSGIServer(app).run()
  sys.exit(ret and 42 or 0)

See the documentation for WSGIServer for more information.

About the bit of logic at the end:
Upon receiving SIGHUP, the python script will exit with status code 42. This
can be used by a wrapper script to determine if the python script should be
re-run. When a SIGINT or SIGTERM is received, the script exits with status
code 0, possibly indicating a normal exit.

Example wrapper script:

Main loop. Call this after instantiating WSGIServer. SIGHUP, SIGINT,

is caught, this method returns True. Returns False otherwise.)

AJP1.3/WSGI server. Runs your WSGI application as a persistant program
that understands AJP1.3. Opens up a TCP socket, binds it, and then
waits for forwarded requests from your webserver.

Why AJP? Two good reasons are that AJP provides load-balancing and
fail-over support. Personally, I just wanted something new to

modules.name: flup.server.fcgi_single
documentation:
fcgi - a FastCGI/WSGI gateway.

For more information about FastCGI, see <http://www.fastcgi.com/>.

For more information about the Web Server Gateway Interface, see
<http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0333.html>.

Example usage:

  #!/usr/bin/env python
  from myapplication import app # Assume app is your WSGI application object
  from fcgi import WSGIServer
  WSGIServer(app).run()

See the documentation for WSGIServer for more information.

On most platforms, fcgi will fallback to regular CGI behavior if run in a
non-FastCGI context. If you want to force CGI behavior, set the environment
variable FCGI_FORCE_CGI to "Y" or "y".

functions.name: flup.server.fcgi_single.test_app
documentation: Probably not the most efficient example.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_single.WSGIServer

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_single.WSGIServer.__init__
documentation:
environ, if present, must be a dictionary-like object. Its
contents will be copied into application's environ. Useful
for passing application-specific variables.

bindAddress, if present, must either be a string or a 2-tuple. If
present, run() will open its own listening socket. You would use
this if you wanted to run your application as an 'external' FastCGI
app. (i.e. the webserver would no longer be responsible for starting
your app) If a string, it will be interpreted as a filename and a UNIX
socket will be opened. If a tuple, the first element, a string,
is the interface name/IP to bind to, and the second element (an int)
is the port number.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_single.WSGIServer.run
documentation:
The main loop. Exits on SIGHUP, SIGINT, SIGTERM. Returns True if
SIGHUP was received, False otherwise.

FastCGI server that supports the Web Server Gateway Interface. See
<http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0333.html>.

Returns errno for select.error, handles differences between Py2 and Py3

\:return: Errno number

classes.name: flup.server.threadpool.ThreadPool

methods.name: flup.server.threadpool.ThreadPool.addJob
documentation:
Adds a job to the work queue. The job object should have a run()
method. If allowQueuing is True (the default), the job will be
added to the work queue regardless if there are any idle threads
ready. (The only way for there to be no idle threads is if maxThreads
is some reasonable, finite limit.)

Otherwise, if allowQueuing is False, and there are no more idle
threads, the job will not be queued.

Returns True if the job was queued, False otherwise.

methods.name: flup.server.threadpool.ThreadPool._worker
documentation: Worker thread routine. Waits for a job, executes it, repeat.

Thread pool that maintains the number of idle threads between
minSpare and maxSpare inclusive. By default, there is no limit on
the number of threads that can be started, but this can be controlled
by maxThreads.

methods.name: flup.server.singleserver.SingleServer.run
documentation:
The main loop. Pass a socket that is ready to accept() client
connections. Return value will be True or False indiciating whether
or not the loop was exited due to SIGHUP.

methods.name: flup.server.singleserver.SingleServer._mainloopPeriodic
documentation:
Called with just about each iteration of the main loop. Meant to
be overridden.

methods.name: flup.server.singleserver.SingleServer._exit
documentation:
Protected convenience method for subclasses to force an exit. Not
really thread-safe, which is why it isn't public.

documentation: Override to provide access control.

methods.name: flup.server.singleserver.SingleServer._hupHandler

modules.name: flup.server.scgi
documentation:
scgi - an SCGI/WSGI gateway.

For more information about SCGI and mod_scgi for Apache1/Apache2, see
<http://www.mems-exchange.org/software/scgi/>.

For more information about the Web Server Gateway Interface, see
<http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0333.html>.

Example usage:

  #!/usr/bin/env python
  import sys
  from myapplication import app # Assume app is your WSGI application object
  from scgi import WSGIServer
  ret = WSGIServer(app).run()
  sys.exit(ret and 42 or 0)

See the documentation for WSGIServer for more information.

About the bit of logic at the end:
Upon receiving SIGHUP, the python script will exit with status code 42. This
can be used by a wrapper script to determine if the python script should be
re-run. When a SIGINT or SIGTERM is received, the script exits with status
code 0, possibly indicating a normal exit.

Example wrapper script:

  #!/bin/sh
  STATUS=42
  while test $STATUS -eq 42; do
    python "$@" that_script_above.py
    STATUS=$?
  done
Copyright (c) 2005, 2006 Allan Saddi <allan@saddi.com>
All rights reserved.
Redistribution and use in source and binary forms, with or without
modification, are permitted provided that the following conditions
are met:

FOR ANY DIRECT, INDIRECT, INCIDENTAL, SPECIAL, EXEMPLARY, OR CONSEQUENTIAL

HOWEVER CAUSED AND ON ANY THEORY OF LIABILITY, WHETHER IN CONTRACT, STRICT
LIABILITY, OR TORT (INCLUDING NEGLIGENCE OR OTHERWISE) ARISING IN ANY WAY
OUT OF THE USE OF THIS SOFTWARE, EVEN IF ADVISED OF THE POSSIBILITY OF
SUCH DAMAGE.
$Id$

functions.name: flup.server.scgi.test_app
documentation: Probably not the most efficient example.

classes.name: flup.server.scgi.WSGIServer

methods.name: flup.server.scgi.WSGIServer.__init__
documentation:
scriptName is the initial portion of the URL path that "belongs"
to your application. It is used to determine PATH_INFO (which doesn't
seem to be passed in). An empty scriptName means your application
is mounted at the root of your virtual host.

environ, which must be a dictionary, can contain any additional
environment variables you want to pass to your application.

bindAddress is the address to bind to, which must be a string or
a tuple of length 2. If a tuple, the first element must be a string,
which is the host name or IPv4 address of a local interface. The
2nd element of the tuple is the port number. If a string, it will
be interpreted as a filename and a UNIX socket will be opened.

If binding to a UNIX socket, umask may be set to specify what
the umask is to be changed to before the socket is created in the
filesystem. After the socket is created, the previous umask is
restored.

allowedServers must be None or a list of strings representing the
IPv4 addresses of servers allowed to connect. None means accept
connections from anywhere.

loggingLevel sets the logging level of the module-level logger.

methods.name: flup.server.scgi.WSGIServer.run
documentation:
Main loop. Call this after instantiating WSGIServer. SIGHUP, SIGINT,
SIGQUIT, SIGTERM cause it to cleanup and return. (If a SIGHUP
is caught, this method returns True. Returns False otherwise.)

SCGI/WSGI server. For information about SCGI (Simple Common Gateway
Interface), see <http://www.mems-exchange.org/software/scgi/>.

This server is similar to SWAP <http://www.idyll.org/~t/www-tools/wsgi/>,
another SCGI/WSGI server.

It differs from SWAP in that it isn't based on scgi.scgi_server and
therefore, it allows me to implement concurrency using threads. (Also,
this server was written from scratch and really has no other depedencies.)
Which server to use really boils down to whether you want multithreading
or forking. (But as an aside, I've found scgi.scgi_server's implementation
of preforking to be quite superior. So if your application really doesn't
mind running in multiple processes, go use SWAP. ;)

modules.name: flup.server.fcgi_fork
documentation:
fcgi - a FastCGI/WSGI gateway.

For more information about FastCGI, see <http://www.fastcgi.com/>.

For more information about the Web Server Gateway Interface, see
<http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0333.html>.

Example usage:

  #!/usr/bin/env python
  from myapplication import app # Assume app is your WSGI application object
  from fcgi import WSGIServer
  WSGIServer(app).run()

See the documentation for WSGIServer for more information.

On most platforms, fcgi will fallback to regular CGI behavior if run in a
non-FastCGI context. If you want to force CGI behavior, set the environment
variable FCGI_FORCE_CGI to "Y" or "y".

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_fork.WSGIServer

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_fork.WSGIServer.__init__
documentation:
environ, if present, must be a dictionary-like object. Its
contents will be copied into application's environ. Useful
for passing application-specific variables.

bindAddress, if present, must either be a string or a 2-tuple. If
present, run() will open its own listening socket. You would use
this if you wanted to run your application as an 'external' FastCGI
app. (i.e. the webserver would no longer be responsible for starting
your app) If a string, it will be interpreted as a filename and a UNIX
socket will be opened. If a tuple, the first element, a string,
is the interface name/IP to bind to, and the second element (an int)
is the port number.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_fork.WSGIServer.run
documentation:
The main loop. Exits on SIGHUP, SIGINT, SIGTERM. Returns True if
SIGHUP was received, False otherwise.

FastCGI server that supports the Web Server Gateway Interface. See
<http://www.python.org/peps/pep-0333.html>.

classes.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer.run
documentation:
The main loop. Pass a socket that is ready to accept() client
connections. Return value will be True or False indiciating whether
or not the loop was exited due to SIGHUP.

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer._cleanupChildren
documentation:
Closes all child sockets (letting those that are available know
that it's time to exit). Sends SIGINT to those that are currently
processing (and hopes that it finishses ASAP).

Any children remaining after 10 seconds is SIGKILLed.

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer._reapChildren
documentation: Cleans up self._children whenever children die.

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer._spawnChild
documentation: Spawn a single child. Returns True if successful, False otherwise.

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer._isClientAllowed
documentation: Override to provide access control.

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer._notifyParent
documentation: Send message to parent, ignoring EPIPE and retrying on EAGAIN

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer._child
documentation: Main loop for children.

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer._hupHandler
documentation: Signal handlers

methods.name: flup.server.preforkserver.PreforkServer._restoreSignalHandlers
documentation: Restores previous signal handlers.

A preforked server model conceptually similar to Apache httpd(2). At
any given time, ensures there are at least minSpare children ready to
process new requests (up to a maximum of maxChildren children total).
If the number of idle children is ever above maxSpare, the extra
children are killed.

If maxRequests is positive, each child will only handle that many
requests in its lifetime before exiting.

jobClass should be a class whose constructor takes at least two
arguments: the client socket and client address. jobArgs, which
must be a list or tuple, is any additional (static) arguments you
wish to pass to the constructor.

jobClass should have a run() method (taking no arguments) that does
the actual work. When run() returns, the request is considered
complete and the child process moves to idle state.

functions.name: flup.server.ajp_base.decodeRequestHeader

functions.name: flup.server.ajp_base.decodeAttribute
documentation: Decode a request attribute.

documentation: Encode a string.

functions.name: flup.server.ajp_base.encodeResponseHeader
documentation: Encode a response header/value pair.

classes.name: flup.server.ajp_base.ProtocolError
documentation:
Exception raised when the server does something unexpected or
sends garbled data. Usually leads to a Connection closing.

classes.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Packet

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Packet._recvall
documentation:
Attempts to receive length bytes from a socket, blocking if necessary.
(Socket may be blocking or non-blocking.)

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Packet.read
documentation: Attempt to read a packet from the server.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Packet._sendall
documentation: Writes data to a socket and does not return until all the data is sent.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Packet.write
documentation: Send a packet to the server.
An AJP message packet.

classes.name: flup.server.ajp_base.InputStream

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.InputStream._shrinkBuffer
documentation: Gets rid of already read data (since we can't rewind).

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.InputStream.setDataLength
documentation: Once Content-Length is known, Request calls this method to set it.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.InputStream.addData
documentation:
Adds data from the server to this InputStream. Note that we never ask
the server for data beyond the Content-Length, so the server should
never send us an EOF (empty string argument).

File-like object that represents the request body (if any). Supports
the bare mininum methods required by the WSGI spec. Thanks to
StringIO for ideas.

classes.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Request

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Request.setMethod
documentation: The following methods are called from the Connection to set up this
Request.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Request.startResponse
documentation:
Begin the HTTP response. This must only be called once and it
must be called before any calls to write().

statusCode is the integer status code (e.g. 200). statusMsg
is the associated reason message (e.g.'OK'). headers is a list
of 2-tuples - header name/value pairs. (Both header name and value
must be strings.)
The only two methods that should be called from the handler.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Request.write
documentation:
Write data (which comprises the response body). Note that due to
restrictions on AJP packet size, we limit our writes to 8185 bytes
each packet.

class_variables.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Request._maxWrite
documentation: 8k - pkt header - send body header - NUL

A Request object. A more fitting name would probably be Transaction, but
it's named Request to mirror my FastCGI driver. :) This object
encapsulates all the data about the HTTP request and allows the handler
to send a response.

The only attributes/methods that the handler should concern itself
with are: environ, input, startResponse(), and write().

classes.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Connection

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Connection.processInput
documentation: Wait for and process a single packet.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Connection._forwardRequest
documentation: Creates a Request object, fills it in from the packet, then runs it.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Connection._shutdown
documentation: Not sure what to do with this yet.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Connection._ping
documentation: I have no idea what this packet means.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Connection._cping
documentation: Respond to a PING (CPING) packet.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Connection._processBody
documentation:
Handles a body chunk from the server by appending it to the
InputStream.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.Connection.writePacket
documentation: Sends a Packet to the server.

A single Connection with the server. Requests are not multiplexed over the
same connection, so at any given time, the Connection is either
waiting for a request, or processing a single request.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.BaseAJPServer.__init__
documentation:
scriptName is the initial portion of the URL path that "belongs"
to your application. It is used to determine PATH_INFO (which doesn't
seem to be passed in). An empty scriptName means your application
is mounted at the root of your virtual host.

environ, which must be a dictionary, can contain any additional
environment variables you want to pass to your application.

Set multithreaded to False if your application is not thread-safe.

Set multiprocess to True to explicitly set wsgi.multiprocess to
True. (Only makes sense with threaded servers.)

bindAddress is the address to bind to, which must be a tuple of
length 2. The first element is a string, which is the host name
or IPv4 address of a local interface. The 2nd element is the port
number.

allowedServers must be None or a list of strings representing the
IPv4 addresses of servers allowed to connect. None means accept
connections from anywhere. By default, it is a list containing
the single item '127.0.0.1'.

loggingLevel sets the logging level of the module-level logger.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.BaseAJPServer._setupSocket
documentation: Creates and binds the socket for communication with the server.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.BaseAJPServer._cleanupSocket
documentation: Closes the main socket.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.BaseAJPServer.handler
documentation:
WSGI handler. Sets up WSGI environment, calls the application,
and sends the application's response.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.BaseAJPServer._sanitizeEnv
documentation: Fill-in/deduce missing values in environ.

methods.name: flup.server.ajp_base.BaseAJPServer.error
documentation:
Override to provide custom error handling. Ideally, however,
all errors should be caught at the application level.

functions.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.decode_pair
documentation:
Decodes a name/value pair.

The number of bytes decoded as well as the name/value pair
are returned.

functions.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.encode_pair
documentation:
Encodes a name/value pair.

The encoded string is returned.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.InputStream

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.InputStream._shrinkBuffer
documentation: Gets rid of already read data (since we can't rewind).

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.InputStream._waitForData
documentation: Waits for more data to become available.

File-like object representing FastCGI input streams (FCGI_STDIN and
FCGI_DATA). Supports the minimum methods required by WSGI spec.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.MultiplexedInputStream
documentation:
A version of InputStream meant to be used with MultiplexedConnections.
Assumes the MultiplexedConnection (the producer) and the Request
(the consumer) are running in different threads.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.OutputStream

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.OutputStream.close
documentation: Sends end-of-stream notification, if necessary.
Though available, the following should NOT be called by WSGI apps.

FastCGI output stream (FCGI_STDOUT/FCGI_STDERR). By default, calls to
write() or writelines() immediately result in Records being sent back
to the server. Buffering should be done in a higher level!

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.TeeOutputStream
documentation:
Simple wrapper around two or more output file-like objects that copies
written data to all streams.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.StdoutWrapper
documentation: Wrapper for sys.stdout so we know if data has actually been written.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Record

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Record._recvall
documentation:
Attempts to receive length bytes from a socket, blocking if necessary.
(Socket may be blocking or non-blocking.)

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Record.read
documentation: Read and decode a Record from a socket.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Record._sendall
documentation: Writes data to a socket and does not return until all the data is sent.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Record.write
documentation: Encode and write a Record to a socket.

A FastCGI Record.

Used for encoding/decoding records.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Request

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Request.run
documentation: Runs the handler, flushes the streams, and ends the request.

Represents a single FastCGI request.

These objects are passed to your handler and is the main interface
between your handler and the fcgi module. The methods should not
be called by your handler. However, server, params, stdin, stdout,
stderr, and data are free for your handler's use.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.CGIRequest
documentation: A normal CGI request disguised as a FastCGI request.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._cleanupSocket
documentation: Close the Connection's socket.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection.run
documentation: Begin processing data from the socket.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection.process_input
documentation: Attempt to read a single Record from the socket and process it.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection.writeRecord
documentation: Write a Record to the socket.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection.end_request
documentation:
End a Request.

Called by Request objects. An FCGI_END_REQUEST Record is
sent to the web server. If the web server no longer requires
the connection, the socket is closed, thereby ending this
Connection (run() returns).

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._do_get_values
documentation: Handle an FCGI_GET_VALUES request from the web server.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._do_begin_request
documentation: Handle an FCGI_BEGIN_REQUEST from the web server.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._do_abort_request
documentation:
Handle an FCGI_ABORT_REQUEST from the web server.

We just mark a flag in the associated Request.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._start_request
documentation: Run the request.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._do_params
documentation:
Handle an FCGI_PARAMS Record.

If the last FCGI_PARAMS Record is received, start the request.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._do_stdin
documentation: Handle the FCGI_STDIN stream.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._do_data
documentation: Handle the FCGI_DATA stream.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.Connection._do_unknown_type
documentation: Handle an unknown request type. Respond accordingly.

A Connection with the web server.

Each Connection is associated with a single socket (which is
connected to the web server) and is responsible for handling all
the FastCGI message processing for that socket.

classes.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.MultiplexedConnection
documentation:
A version of Connection capable of handling multiple requests
simultaneously.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.BaseFCGIServer.__init__
documentation:
bindAddress, if present, must either be a string or a 2-tuple. If
present, run() will open its own listening socket. You would use
this if you wanted to run your application as an 'external' FastCGI
app. (i.e. the webserver would no longer be responsible for starting
your app) If a string, it will be interpreted as a filename and a UNIX
socket will be opened. If a tuple, the first element, a string,
is the interface name/IP to bind to, and the second element (an int)
is the port number.

If binding to a UNIX socket, umask may be set to specify what
the umask is to be changed to before the socket is created in the
filesystem. After the socket is created, the previous umask is
restored.

Set multiplexed to True if you want to handle multiple requests
per connection. Some FastCGI backends (namely mod_fastcgi) don't
multiplex requests at all, so by default this is off (which saves
on thread creation/locking overhead). If threads aren't available,
this keyword is ignored; it's not possible to multiplex requests
at all.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.BaseFCGIServer._cleanupSocket
documentation: Closes the main socket.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.BaseFCGIServer.handler
documentation: Special handler for WSGI.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.BaseFCGIServer._sanitizeEnv
documentation: Ensure certain values are present, if required by WSGI.

methods.name: flup.server.fcgi_base.BaseFCGIServer.error
documentation:
Called by Request if an exception occurs within the handler. May and
should be overridden.

modules.name: flup.client.scgi_app
documentation: Copyright (c) 2006 Allan Saddi <allan@saddi.com>

functions.name: flup.client.fcgi_app.decode_pair
documentation:
Decodes a name/value pair.

The number of bytes decoded as well as the name/value pair
are returned.

functions.name: flup.client.fcgi_app.encode_pair
documentation:
Encodes a name/value pair.

The encoded string is returned.

classes.name: flup.client.fcgi_app.Record

methods.name: flup.client.fcgi_app.Record._recvall
documentation:
Attempts to receive length bytes from a socket, blocking if necessary.
(Socket may be blocking or non-blocking.)

methods.name: flup.client.fcgi_app.Record.read
documentation: Read and decode a Record from a socket.

methods.name: flup.client.fcgi_app.Record._sendall
documentation: Writes data to a socket and does not return until all the data is sent.

methods.name: flup.client.fcgi_app.Record.write
documentation: Encode and write a Record to a socket.

A FastCGI Record.

Used for encoding/decoding records.
